patterns.name: AI-Agent Solo Venture Launch
patterns.summary: An 8-week government-backed structured program that trains solo founders to use AI agents as co-founders across every phase of company building — planning, development, marketing, and operations. Participants progress through three stages: broad agent-design education, one-on-one technical mentorship paired with business-model validation, and a hackathon-to-seed-funding sprint. It is one of the very few programs whose explicit mission is the agent-powered one-person company, not just AI tool adoption.
patterns.intent: Train a solo founder to design, deploy, and operate an AI agent stack that substitutes for a founding team across all core business functions.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use this track when a prospective founder has domain expertise and a business idea but lacks a technical co-founder, and when the program operator wants to produce agent-native solo companies at national scale rather than merely AI-literate individuals.

inputs.name: Solo founder applicant
inputs.description: A motivated individual with a business idea or domain expertise who has applied and been selected nationally; no prior coding or AI background required.

inputs.name: Agent design curriculum
inputs.description: Faculty-authored materials covering AI agent custom design, orchestration concepts, and practical integration for business tasks.

inputs.name: Technical mentor pool
inputs.description: Graduate-level AI researchers (e.g., KAIST PhD and Master's students) available for one-on-one technical pairing with each cohort team.

inputs.name: Investor and expert network
inputs.description: VC professionals and startup experts who review business models and provide validation feedback at Stage 2.

inputs.name: Seed fund
inputs.description: A pool of government or institutional capital (e.g., up to 150M KRW per team) ready to deploy to the best-performing teams at Stage 3.

outputs.name: Agent-enabled solo founder
outputs.description: A founder who can independently scope, build, and operate a multi-agent system covering at least one full business function.

outputs.name: Running agent-operated business
outputs.description: A live company with an AI agent stack handling planning, development, marketing, or operations — the masterpiece that demonstrates the principal skill was earned.

outputs.name: Validated business model
outputs.description: A pitch-ready business model reviewed by investors and refined through mentor feedback.

outputs.name: Seed investment
outputs.description: Funding awarded to the top cohort teams to commercialize their agent-powered venture.

steps.name: Stage 1 — AI Base Camp
steps.description: Faculty deliver agent custom-design and practical application education to the full cohort of approximately 100 participants. Founders learn to identify which business functions agents can own, design agent roles, and connect agents to real business data and workflows. Selection spans both metropolitan and regional participants to ensure national breadth.

steps.name: Stage 2 — AI Working Group
steps.description: The cohort narrows to approximately 50 teams, each matched one-on-one with a KAIST AI graduate student for technical advancement. In parallel, VC and startup experts run a business-model review session for each team. Founders iterate on their agent architecture while validating the business hypothesis with external scrutiny.

steps.name: Stage 3 — Scale-up Hackathon and Pitch
steps.description: The top 25 teams compete in a hackathon format, then pitch to a panel. Winners receive seed investment (up to 100M KRW per founder) and commercialization support. The hackathon tests whether the agent stack actually runs under competitive, time-boxed conditions.
produces: pitch deck with live agent demo

principles: Treat AI agents as co-founders, not tools — the agent owns a role, not just a task.
Compress the founding team to one person by systematically replacing each team function with an agent before hiring.
Evidence gates advancement — each stage filters on demonstrated agent capability, not intent.
National reach requires geographic distribution from day one; diversity of context sharpens agent designs.
patterns.threshold: The founder can demo a running agent that autonomously handles at least one complete business function (sales, support, content, or development) and can explain why the agent design choice fits the business model.
patterns.masterpiece: A live, revenue-seeking business operated by a single founder where an AI agent stack performs at least one full department's work, demonstrated under hackathon conditions to an investor panel.

known_uses.name: 머니투데이 launch coverage
known_uses.quote: AI 에이전트를 활용해 기획·개발·마케팅·운영 등 창업 전 과정을 1인이 수행할 수 있도록 훈련하는
known_uses.note: Verbatim program description from announcement article

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of rebranding AI tool adoption as agent-native founding. If founders exit knowing how to prompt ChatGPT but not how to design autonomous agent workflows, the program produced operators, not principals.
When selection at each stage filters out non-technical candidates rather than those with weak agent designs, the program drifts into a technical bootcamp and loses its founding mission.

The anti-pattern of polishing a pitch rather than running a real agent stack. Hackathon pressure can push teams to demo a prototype and describe it as operational before it actually handles real business transactions.

sources.title: AI를 공동창업자로 — KAIST, 에이전트 활용 전국 1인 창업 시대 연다
sources.quote: AI 에이전트를 활용해 기획·개발·마케팅·운영 등 창업 전 과정을 1인이 수행할 수 있도록 훈련하는

sources.title: KAIST OverEdge official site
sources.quote: 세계 최고 수준의 교수진이 전수하는 AI 에이전트 커스텀 설계 및 실전 적용 교육

sources.title: KAIST AI로 혼자 기획·개발 — KAIST 솔로프러너 키운다

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Solo founder applicant
with business idea] --> B[Stage 1: AI Base Camp

    B --> C{Selection

patterns.example_scenario: A 28-year-old logistics domain expert from Daejeon applies to KAIST OverEdge with a sketch for an AI-powered freight matching service. She has no coding background. In Stage 1, she learns to design an agent that monitors freight board APIs, scores loads against her customer's preferences, and auto-drafts acceptance messages. Her mentor in Stage 2 is a KAIST PhD student specializing in multi-agent systems; together they extend the agent to handle rate negotiation by email. An investor review session reveals that her unit economics only work if the agent also handles invoice generation, so she adds a third agent. By the hackathon, her stack processes 40 freight matches per day with no human review. She pitches a live demo, wins seed funding, and signs her first two paying freight brokers within 30 days of program exit. The masterpiece is a running business — not a slide deck — operated by one person.

patterns.name: Solo Founder Venture Sprint
patterns.summary: A 3-month in-person accelerator in San Francisco for solo founders building technology companies. Small cohorts of ten founders receive early-stage investment, weekly one-on-one strategic mentorship, community infrastructure, and significant cloud and AI partner credits. The program provides the structure and social scaffolding that a founding team normally supplies — it does not deliver an AI agent curriculum. Its value for the principal step is in demonstrating that the solo-founder model is viable and fundable, and in providing access to AI platform credits that enable agent experimentation.
patterns.intent: Provide a solo founder with the community, capital, strategic mentorship, and AI infrastructure access needed to reach first traction without a co-founder.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use this track when a solo founder has a clear technology product direction and needs accountability structure, investor-ready framing, and peer community rather than agent-design education. It is the right choice when the founder already has agent-building capability and needs business scaffolding, not when they need to learn how to architect an agent stack.

inputs.name: Solo founder with technology product direction
inputs.description: A selected founder who is building a technology company alone and has enough product clarity to benefit from weekly strategic review.

inputs.name: Program operator with investor network
inputs.description: Julian Weisser or equivalent — a mentor with deep network access, prior accelerator experience, and relationships with investors and operators.

inputs.name: Cohort of 10 peers
inputs.description: Co-founders-by-community: other solo founders at a similar stage who provide weekly accountability, peer feedback, and shared learning.

inputs.name: AI platform partner credits
inputs.description: 800K+ USD in credits from Anthropic, OpenAI, AWS, and other AI infrastructure providers, lowering the cost of building agent-based products.

outputs.name: Venture-ready solo founder
outputs.description: A founder with a refined product narrative, investor relationships, and demonstrated ability to operate independently across product, sales, and fundraising.

outputs.name: Running agent-assisted technology company
outputs.description: A live product with early traction — the masterpiece — potentially leveraging AI agents for core operations given the partner credits provided.

outputs.name: Seed-stage funding or YC acceptance
outputs.description: A concrete next financing milestone, evidenced by alumni outcomes including YC acceptance and multi-million-dollar raises.

steps.name: Cohort kickoff and priority setting
steps.description: A kickoff retreat establishes community norms, surfaces each founder's primary constraint, and sets the first weekly goals. Julian Weisser runs an initial one-on-one session to understand the founder's product, market, and personal working style.

steps.name: Weekly one-on-one strategic review
steps.description: Each week the founder meets with Julian to review progress against the single most important goal, unblock specific obstacles, and reset priorities. The format is deliberately narrow — one priority at a time — to prevent the context-switching that kills solo founders.

steps.name: Operator and investor exposure
steps.description: Dinners with experienced operators and office hours with investors give the founder direct access to people who have solved the specific problems they face. Fundraising strategy and sales motion are covered through structured sessions.

steps.name: Traction milestone and alumni transition
steps.description: By the end of three months, the founder targets a concrete traction milestone — first paying customer, first cohort of users, or a funding event. Alumni remain connected to the network for continued peer support and warm introductions.

principles: One priority at a time — the solo founder's scarcest resource is focused attention, not effort.
Community replaces the co-founder's accountability function; peer pressure and shared ambition substitute for a partner.
AI credits are infrastructure, not curriculum — the program removes cost barriers but does not teach agent architecture.
Track record of funded alumni is the program's primary evidence standard; outcomes beat promises.
patterns.threshold: The founder reaches a concrete traction milestone — first paying customer, product launch with user feedback, or a fundraising commitment — by the end of three months.
patterns.masterpiece: A live technology product with demonstrable early traction, operated by a single founder, that a credible investor has agreed to fund or that has reached its first revenue milestone.

known_uses.name: Solo Founders Program
known_uses.quote: Super lucky to be a part of Solo. I've seen a shift in the past 4 months where AI is empowering the individual so much I'm almost positive solo founding will be the new default.
known_uses.note: Alumni quote; program itself does not teach AI agent workflows as curriculum

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of a founder who optimizes for the weekly meeting rather than for customer and product learning. When the mentor becomes the primary audience, the program produces well-briefed presenters rather than operating founders.

The anti-pattern of substituting peer belonging for skill development. Solo Founders Program provides community but not agent-design education; founders who need both cannot get the agent-architecture dimension here.

800K+ in AI platform credits are only useful if the founder has the technical capability to consume them. Without agent-design training, the credits go to waste and the principal-step value of the AI infrastructure advantage is never realized.

sources.title: Solo Founders Program — program page
sources.quote: Julian co-founded On Deck/ODF and has helped thousands start companies worth over $10B across 27 cohorts.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Solo founder
with product direction] --> B[Cohort kickoff
retreat + priority setting]
    B --> C[Weekly 1-on-1
strategic review
with Julian Weisser]
    C --> D[Operator dinners
+ investor office hours]
    D --> E{Traction
milestone reached?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Alumni network
+ follow-on funding]
    E -- No --> C
    F --> G[Funded solo-founder
technology company]

patterns.example_scenario: A former enterprise software sales lead leaves her job to build a B2B AI content tool aimed at mid-market marketing teams. She is a strong product thinker but has never run a company alone. She is accepted into a Solo Founders Program cohort of ten. At kickoff, her stated constraint is that she keeps rebuilding the product instead of selling. Julian sets a single weekly goal: one sales conversation per day, no building allowed until it happens. Over three months, she conducts 60 customer conversations, discovers her initial positioning is wrong, and pivots to a narrower use case. By week 10 she has three paying pilot customers. She uses her Anthropic credits to wire together a Claude-based content generation agent that replaces the manual drafting step her customers hated. By month three she has $8K MRR and a term sheet from a pre-seed fund she met through the program's investor dinner. The masterpiece is the live product with paying customers — not the agent architecture, which she had to learn independently — illustrating both the program's strength (business scaffolding) and its gap (no agent curriculum).

patterns.name: Solo Operator AI Agent Team Build
patterns.summary: A self-paced French-language course that teaches a solo business operator to build a personal AI agent team without writing code. Four modules take the learner from designing an AI org chart, through centralizing business knowledge in a shared Notion base, to customizing ten pre-built agent templates covering sales, content, and delegation roles. Community iteration closes the loop. It is the only French-language training found that explicitly frames the output as an operating AI team for a one-person business.
patterns.intent: Give a solo operator a working set of customized AI agents that cover the key roles in their business, without requiring coding skills.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use this move when a freelancer or solopreneur already has a running business and wants to scale their output by delegating repeatable work to agents — not when the goal is to launch a new venture or learn agent architecture. Best applied when the operator has a clear picture of their repetitive weekly tasks and is willing to invest time documenting their business context in a knowledge base.

inputs.name: Solo operator with a running business
inputs.description: A freelancer, consultant, or solopreneur who has existing clients or revenue and wants to increase capacity without hiring.

inputs.name: Business context documentation
inputs.description: The operator's existing prompts, standard operating procedures, client communication templates, and brand voice — the raw material for the Notion knowledge base.

inputs.name: Ten pre-built agent templates
inputs.description: Provided by the course: named agent roles for sales proposals, LinkedIn content, landing page copy, delegation workflows, and automation tasks — each requiring customization rather than build-from-scratch.

inputs.name: Notion workspace
inputs.description: A no-code knowledge base tool used to centralize all agent context so agents can pull from consistent business data.

outputs.name: More capable solo operator
outputs.description: An operator who can independently design new agent roles, customize templates, and evaluate agent output against business standards.

outputs.name: Running personal AI agent team
outputs.description: A set of ten or more live agents handling sales, content, and operational tasks for the operator's business — the masterpiece of this move.

outputs.name: Centralized agent knowledge base
outputs.description: A Notion workspace containing all SOPs, prompts, and brand context that makes agent outputs consistent and maintainable.

steps.name: Module 1 — Design your AI org chart
steps.description: Map the operator's business functions against potential agent roles. Identify which tasks are repetitive, well-defined, and safe to delegate. The output is a named org chart: e.g., Sales Agent, LinkedIn Content Agent, Client Onboarding Agent.

steps.name: Module 2 — Build the agent knowledge base
steps.description: Centralize all prompts, SOPs, brand voice guidelines, and company context in a structured Notion workspace. Each agent will draw from this single source of truth, keeping outputs consistent as the business evolves.
produces: Notion knowledge base with business context

steps.name: Module 3 — Customize ten agent templates
steps.description: Work through each of the ten pre-built templates, injecting business-specific context from the knowledge base. Test each agent against real tasks. Templates cover: sales proposal drafting, LinkedIn content generation, landing page copy, delegation automation, and more.

steps.name: Module 4 — Community iteration
steps.description: Share agent outputs in the community, receive peer feedback, refine prompts and workflows. The community replaces the co-founder review loop that would exist in a team-based business.

principles: Design roles before building agents — an org chart forces clarity about what each agent owns.
A shared knowledge base is the backbone of a consistent agent team; without it, agents drift into generic output.
No-code first: the operator's business insight is more valuable than technical depth at this stage.
Community iteration compensates for the absence of a team; peer review catches what solo operators miss.
patterns.threshold: The operator runs a full working week where at least three agents handle tasks they previously did manually, and can describe what each agent owns and where the agent's limit is.
patterns.masterpiece: A live personal AI agent team of at least ten named agents — covering sales, content, and operational roles — running on real business tasks, built and maintained by the operator without coding.

known_uses.name: Solo Squad® — Agents IA pour solopreneurs
known_uses.quote: tu apprends à créer tes propres agents ou assistants IA personnalisés pour ton business
known_uses.note: Self-paced, no-code, French-language; teaches operating an AI team, not founding a venture

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of deploying agent templates without populating the knowledge base. Agents produce generic output that the operator ends up correcting manually, defeating the delegation goal.
Creating ten agents because the course says ten, not because ten distinct roles exist in the business. Unused agents become maintenance debt.

The anti-pattern of treating no-code tools as sufficient for all agent tasks. When a business need requires custom logic, API integration, or multi-agent orchestration, the operator hits a capability wall they were not trained to cross.

sources.title: Solo Squad® — crée tes agents IA et automatise ton business solo (sans coder)
sources.quote: tu apprends à créer tes propres agents ou assistants IA personnalisés pour ton business

diagram.mermaid:

solo operator]

patterns.example_scenario: A French-speaking independent marketing consultant runs a one-person agency serving five SME clients. She spends roughly 12 hours a week drafting LinkedIn posts, writing sales proposals, and onboarding new clients through repetitive emails. She enrolls in Solo Squad. In Module 1 she maps her business and identifies four agent roles: LinkedIn Content Agent, Sales Proposal Agent, Client Onboarding Agent, and Monthly Report Agent. In Module 2 she spends two sessions building a Notion knowledge base containing her brand voice, her five clients' industries and preferences, her proposal template structure, and her onboarding checklist. In Module 3 she customizes the four relevant templates from the ten provided, testing each against last month's real client work and comparing output to what she would have written. By Module 4 she has agents handling the first draft of every LinkedIn post and proposal. Community feedback helps her refine the tone of the Sales Proposal Agent after peers note it sounds too formal for her target clients. After four weeks she has reclaimed eight hours per week, which she reinvests in two additional client pitches. The masterpiece is a live, operating agent team — four agents, real tasks, real clients — maintained in Notion with no code.

patterns.name: AI-Enabled MVP Launch Sprint
patterns.summary: A two-week live online bootcamp that takes a non-technical founder from idea to a working, customer-tested MVP using AI agents and no-code tools — without writing a single line of code. Participants set up an AI tool system, launch a first product, collect real customer feedback, and receive live mentor and peer review. It covers the earliest slice of the principal step: idea to first live product. It stops before sustained agent-run operations or revenue scale, and does not teach agent architecture or multi-agent orchestration.
patterns.intent: Take a non-technical founder from idea to a live, customer-tested MVP in two weeks using AI agents and no-code tools.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use this move when a first-time or non-technical founder has a validated hypothesis but cannot build a product independently, and when the goal is proof of concept and first customer feedback rather than a production-grade agent stack. It is the right entry point when the barrier is build confidence, not business model clarity.

inputs.name: Non-technical founder with a business hypothesis
inputs.description: A founder who has an idea to test, domain expertise or market insight, and the motivation to ship a first product — but no coding background.

inputs.name: AI tool system setup guidance
inputs.description: Bootcamp-provided instruction on which no-code and AI tools to combine (e.g., automation platforms, AI content generators, form builders) and how to connect them into a working product.

inputs.name: Mentor and expert access
inputs.description: Live feedback sessions with mentors and domain experts during the two-week program who review product scoping decisions and early customer responses.

inputs.name: Peer cohort
inputs.description: A group of fellow builders who provide accountability, honest feedback on product clarity, and shared problem-solving during the sprint.

outputs.name: AI-enabled MVP founder
outputs.description: A founder who can independently scope, build, and launch a no-code MVP using AI tools, and who understands how to set up a basic AI system of integrations and automations.

outputs.name: Live MVP with customer feedback
outputs.description: A working first product tested with real customers — the masterpiece — demonstrating that the hypothesis can be validated without a technical co-founder.

outputs.name: AI tool system
outputs.description: A configured set of integrations and automations that power the MVP, reusable as the foundation for the next iteration.

steps.name: Scope the MVP
steps.description: Define the single most valuable hypothesis to test and the minimum feature set needed to test it. Use AI tools to draft a product brief, identify the target user, and scope the build to what can be completed in one week.

steps.name: Build the AI tool system
steps.description: Configure the AI system: connect no-code tools, set up automations, and wire together the AI integrations that make the product work. Founder Institute provides tool recommendations and setup walkthroughs. Target: a working product by end of week one.

steps.name: Launch and collect customer feedback
steps.description: Put the product in front of real users. Track signups, usage, and explicit feedback. Mentor sessions review what the data says about the hypothesis. Peer cohort gives product feedback from a fresh perspective.

steps.name: Iterate and present
steps.description: Make one meaningful improvement based on customer feedback. Present findings to the cohort and mentors: what worked, what did not, and what the next iteration would be.

principles: Domain expertise is the unfair advantage; AI tools remove the technical barrier that would otherwise block it.
Ship by end of week one — waiting for a perfect product is the most common solo-founder failure mode.
A hypothesis is only tested by real customers, not by mentor approval or peer enthusiasm.
No-code first means faster iteration, not permanent limitation — the goal is evidence, not architecture.
patterns.threshold: The founder has a live product that at least five real users have tried, and can articulate one thing the product confirmed and one thing it disproved about the original hypothesis.
patterns.masterpiece: A live, accessible no-code MVP — built and launched by a non-technical founder in two weeks — with documented first-customer feedback that either validates or invalidates the founding hypothesis.

known_uses.name: Vibe Coding Bootcamp
known_uses.quote: Set up your AI 'system' of tools, integrations, and automations
known_uses.note: 2-week program; targets non-technical first-time founders; stops at MVP launch, not sustained agent-run business

known_uses.name: Founder Institute main site
known_uses.quote: No Co-Founder? No Problem. AI tools and agents can get you to first traction and funding.
known_uses.note: Homepage positioning as AI-native company builder

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of spending week one building rather than scoping, then running out of time to collect real customer feedback. Without a strict scope gate at day two, the bootcamp becomes a build marathon rather than a validation sprint.
The anti-pattern of treating positive mentor feedback as proof that the hypothesis works. Mentor approval and customer evidence are not equivalent; the bootcamp succeeds only when real users respond.

Choosing the AI tools the bootcamp recommends rather than the ones that fit the business. Early tool choices constrain later architecture; founders who do not evaluate tradeoffs carry technical debt into the next stage.

sources.title: Vibe Coding Bootcamp: Scope, Launch, and Validate Your MVP Using AI Tools
sources.quote: scope, build, and launch a working MVP in just two weeks — without needing to write a single line of code

sources.title: Founder Institute — World's Largest AI-Native Company Builder
sources.quote: Skills are no longer a barrier to entry. Your domain expertise is your unfair advantage, but only if you become AI-enabled.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Non-technical founder
with business hypothesis] --> B[Scope MVP
define hypothesis + feature set]
    B --> C[Build AI tool system
no-code + automations]
    C --> D[Launch to real users]
    D --> E[Collect customer feedback
+ mentor review]
    E --> F{Hypothesis
tested?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Iterate once
+ present findings]
    F -- No --> D
    G --> H[Live MVP with
customer evidence]

patterns.example_scenario: A healthcare administrator with ten years of experience in patient billing notices that small dental practices waste hours each week reconciling insurance claim rejections manually. She has no coding background. She joins the Founder Institute Vibe Coding Bootcamp with a hypothesis: a simple AI-assisted rejection analysis tool would save dental office managers two hours per week. In day one she scopes the MVP to a single function — upload a rejection file, get an AI-generated explanation of the top three reasons and suggested fixes — using no-code tools and an AI API integration. By end of week one she has a working tool hosted on a no-code platform. She contacts five dental office managers she knows from her network and asks them to try it. Four of the five use it within 48 hours. Their feedback: the explanations are useful but the output needs to be formatted for printing and sharing with the billing team. She makes one formatting improvement. In the final mentor session, she presents: hypothesis confirmed on time savings, new requirement discovered on output format, next step is to add a PDF export. The masterpiece is the live tool with five real users and documented feedback — shipped in two weeks without a single line of code.

patterns.name: AI-First Venture Build
patterns.summary: A training Move — currently an aspiration with thin real-world evidence — that would take a founder from idea to a revenue-positive business where AI agents handle the work of at least one full department. It is distinguished from existing accelerators by teaching agent architecture and orchestration as the core operational model, not as a productivity layer on top of a human team. No verified program delivering this end-to-end curriculum exists as of mid-2026. The closest real approximation is KAIST OverEdge (Korean, government-funded, just launching) and isolated self-taught success stories.
patterns.intent: Train a founder to design, deploy, and iterate on a multi-agent stack that replaces at least one department's worth of human work, taking the business from idea to first revenue.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply this move when a founder wants to build a company where agents are the operating model, not just tools — and when an appropriate structured program exists in their ecosystem. As of mid-2026, this move is largely self-directed; practitioners who need this training must assemble their own curriculum from adjacent programs and public practitioner accounts. It becomes the right primary track as purpose-built programs emerge.

inputs.name: Founder with a business idea and agent-design motivation
inputs.description: A founder who explicitly wants to build a company whose operations run on agents, not just a company that uses AI products.

inputs.name: Agent architecture curriculum
inputs.description: Materials covering multi-agent orchestration, agent role design, tool integration, monitoring, and failure modes — currently assembled from public sources, not delivered by a single verified program.

inputs.name: Real business problem to solve
inputs.description: A specific department function (sales, support, content, code review) that the founder commits to replacing with an agent stack by the end of the move.

inputs.name: Revenue target
inputs.description: A concrete first-revenue milestone that forces the agent stack to perform under real commercial conditions, not just in demo environments.

outputs.name: Agent-native founder
outputs.description: A founder who can independently design, deploy, monitor, and iterate on a multi-agent system that replaces a human department's work.

outputs.name: Revenue-positive agent-operated business
outputs.description: A live company that has reached its first revenue milestone with an agent stack performing at least one full department's work — the masterpiece of this move.

outputs.name: Agent operations playbook
outputs.description: Documented decisions: which functions were agent-replaced, which were kept human, what the escalation and monitoring setup looks like, and what it cost versus the headcount alternative.

steps.name: Agent-stack scoping
steps.description: Map the target business across its core functions. For each function, assess whether it is agent-replaceable (well-defined, repeatable, verifiable output) or human-required (judgment, relationship, context). Commit to one department as the first full agent replacement target.

steps.name: First agent build and real-customer test
steps.description: Design and deploy the first agent for the target function. Wire it to real data and real customer touchpoints. Measure output quality against what a human would produce. Iterate until the agent handles the function without human review for routine cases.

steps.name: Orchestration layer and department replacement
steps.description: Add the orchestration layer that connects agent outputs into a coherent department operation. Replace the human workflow step by step, measuring cost per outcome versus the headcount baseline. Reach the milestone where the target department runs on agents.

steps.name: Revenue milestone sprint
steps.description: Drive the business to first revenue with the agent stack operational. The revenue milestone forces real-world performance testing that no demo environment can substitute. Document what broke, what required human escalation, and what the next agent replacement target is.

steps.name: Governance and monitoring setup
steps.description: Establish guardrails, monitoring dashboards, and human escalation paths for autonomous agent operations. Define what constitutes an agent failure, who reviews it, and how agent behavior is audited over time.

principles: Replace departments, not tasks — an agent that assists a human is a productivity tool; an agent that owns a function is an operational model.
Revenue is the only test that matters — an agent stack that works in demos but fails under commercial pressure has not been built.
Measure cost per outcome against a headcount baseline; without this comparison the principal-step claim is unverified.
Governance is not optional — autonomous agent operations without monitoring and escalation paths are a liability, not an asset.
patterns.threshold: The founder has reached first revenue with an agent stack handling at least one department's full workload, has documented the cost-per-outcome versus headcount comparison, and has a monitoring and escalation system in place.
patterns.masterpiece: A revenue-positive business where an AI agent stack performs the work of at least one full department, operated by a single founder, with a documented governance and monitoring setup.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of claiming an agent-native business while still relying on human review of every agent output. If a human checks every agent decision before it reaches a customer, the department has not been replaced — the agent is a drafting tool.

The anti-pattern of treating positive investor reaction or mentor approval as equivalent to first revenue. The principal step is only earned when a real customer pays for output the agent produced autonomously.

Deploying autonomous agents into commercial operations without monitoring, escalation paths, or audit trails. When an agent fails in a customer interaction, the founder needs a documented response — improvising under pressure is not a governance model.

patterns.ecosystem: global (gap — no single ecosystem owns this)

patterns.example_scenario:
Note: this pattern is a stub — no verified cohort program delivers this curriculum end-to-end as of mid-2026. The following scenario is drawn from self-taught practitioner accounts, not program alumni.

A solo founder in B2B SaaS decides to build a customer support operation run entirely by agents. He has no support team and no budget to hire one. He maps the support function: ticket triage, first-response drafting, escalation to human (himself) for edge cases, and weekly report generation. He builds a triage agent connected to his helpdesk API that classifies tickets by topic and urgency. He adds a response drafting agent using a fine-tuned prompt library built from his own past responses. After two weeks of supervised operation, he sets the stack to respond autonomously to routine tickets (60% of volume) and escalate the rest. At month two he has 200 active customers and a support agent handling 120 tickets per week with a 94% satisfaction score — identical to his own score when he did it manually. His cost per support ticket is $0.12 versus the $8.50 industry average for human agents. He has reached first revenue milestone ($15K MRR) with the agent stack operational. The masterpiece exists; the program to teach others how to reach it does not yet.

patterns.name: Agent-Native Startup Cohort
patterns.summary: A track — currently an identified gap, not a verified program — for a multi-week cohort that explicitly teaches founding and operating a business whose core production capacity runs on an AI agent stack. No such global English-language program was found as of mid-2026. Major accelerators (Google for Startups, Antler, YC, Techstars) accept and support AI-product startups but focus their curriculum on building AI products for external customers, not on training founders to run their own operations via agents. KAIST OverEdge (Korean, government-funded) is the only nationally-scoped program matching this definition.
patterns.intent: Run a multi-week cohort that trains founders to design, build, and operate a business whose core production functions are handled by an AI agent stack, from idea to first revenue.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply this track when a program operator wants to produce agent-native founders at cohort scale — not just AI-literate entrepreneurs. Use it when the operator has access to agent-architecture mentors, real business problem contexts, and the infrastructure to support cohort-level live agent builds. As of mid-2026, program operators considering this track must build it themselves; no off-the-shelf version exists in English.

inputs.name: Cohort of 10-20 founders with business ideas
inputs.description: Founders with enough domain knowledge to identify agent-replaceable functions in a specific market or industry.

inputs.name: Agent-architecture mentor faculty
inputs.description: Practitioners or researchers with direct experience designing and operating multi-agent systems in commercial settings — the critical missing resource in most existing accelerators.

inputs.name: 12-week structured curriculum
inputs.description: A week-by-week program covering agent-stack scoping, first agent build, orchestration, revenue milestone, and governance — as described in the AI-First Venture Build move but delivered in cohort format with peer accountability.

inputs.name: AI infrastructure access
inputs.description: Cloud credits, API access, and tool licenses so cohort founders can build and run real agent stacks without cost barriers blocking experimentation.

inputs.name: Investor and commercial partner network
inputs.description: Investors who understand agent-native businesses and commercial partners willing to be first customers for cohort agent stacks — both are required to make the revenue milestone stage credible.

outputs.name: Agent-native founders
outputs.description: A cohort of founders who can independently design, deploy, and operate multi-agent stacks as their primary business operating model.

outputs.name: Portfolio of agent-operated startups
outputs.description: 10-20 early-stage companies, each with a live agent stack handling at least one department's work and a documented path to first revenue — the collective masterpiece of the cohort.

outputs.name: Agent-native playbook
outputs.description: A documented body of decisions, failure modes, and governance patterns from the cohort that can inform future cohorts and the broader practitioner community.

steps.name: Weeks 1-2: Agent-stack scoping
steps.description: Each founder maps their target business across core functions and identifies which are agent-replaceable versus human-required. Cohort workshop format with cross-founder critique. Output: a committed agent replacement roadmap for each founder.

steps.name: Weeks 3-4: First agent build with real customers
steps.description: Each founder builds their first production agent and tests it with real customers or users. Agent-architecture mentors provide technical review. Peer cohort gives feedback on product fit and agent scope.

steps.name: Weeks 5-8: Orchestration and department replacement
steps.description: Founders add orchestration layers and progress toward full department replacement. Weekly cohort reviews compare cost-per-outcome data across the group. Shared failure modes are documented and circulated as cohort learning.

steps.name: Weeks 9-12: Revenue sprint and investor readiness
steps.description: Founders drive to first revenue with the agent stack as the core narrative. Investor readiness sessions frame the agent stack as the business's defensible operating model, not just a cost-saving feature. Cohort culminates in a demo day where live agent stacks are shown — not slide decks.

principles: Agent stack first, team second — the cohort's goal is companies whose agent architecture is the moat, not a feature.
Revenue is the proof gate — no founder advances past week eight without a live agent handling real commercial traffic.
Cohort learning amplifies individual builds — failure modes discovered by one founder become curriculum for all.
Demo day shows live systems, not decks — investor conversations are grounded in running agent stacks, not projections.
patterns.threshold: Every cohort founder can demonstrate a live agent stack handling at least one production business function with real customer traffic, and has reached or credibly projected a first revenue milestone.
patterns.masterpiece: A cohort-level portfolio of agent-native startups, each with a running multi-agent stack, first revenue, and a documented governance model — presented to investors at a demo day that shows live systems.

known_uses.name: KAIST OverEdge (nearest analog, Korean only)
known_uses.note: Korean, government-funded, just launching — closest real-world match but not a global cohort

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of the cohort drifting into training founders to build AI products for customers rather than training them to run their own operations on agents. This is the pattern every major existing accelerator has fallen into; it requires active curriculum correction.

The anti-pattern of advancing founders to investor readiness without a revenue milestone gate. Agent-native pitch decks without live revenue data are indistinguishable from any other AI startup narrative; the principal step requires the real thing.

Staffing the cohort with AI product managers or ML engineers rather than practitioners who have operated multi-agent stacks in commercial settings. The missing mentor type — the agent-native operator — is what makes this cohort hard to run in 2026.

patterns.example_scenario:
Note: this pattern is a stub — no verified global English-language cohort program delivers this curriculum as of mid-2026. The following scenario describes what a well-run instance would look like, based on the KAIST OverEdge model and the self-taught practitioner accounts in the field.

A program operator at a global startup accelerator decides to run a 12-week agent-native cohort as a pilot. She recruits 12 founders from her alumni network — all have prior startup experience but none have built agent stacks. She brings in three mentors who have run commercial agent systems: a founder who built a content agency on agents, an engineer who designed a customer support agent stack at scale, and a VC who has specifically funded agent-native companies. Week one: all 12 founders map their target business functions and commit to a first agent replacement target. By week four, eight founders have a production agent handling real customer interactions. By week eight, five have measured their cost-per-outcome against a human baseline and all five show a better-than-2x improvement. By week twelve, four founders have first revenue with the agent stack operational. Demo day shows live systems — agents processing real orders, responding to real support tickets, generating real sales proposals — not pitch decks. Two of the four revenue-stage founders close pre-seed rounds at the event. The cohort playbook documents seven shared failure modes that become the curriculum improvement for cohort two. The masterpiece is the portfolio — four funded, agent-native startups — and the playbook that can teach the next cohort faster.
